dead.quote: I told them the truth — both <Werewolf> cards were in the center, no wolves among the living. I was the <Minion>, and my claim was genuine. They executed me anyway, and found exactly what I said: nothing. No team wins. Beautiful, isn't it? The village murdered the one person telling them the truth, and the wolves were never even in the room to begin with.

losers.quote: We voted out 攪局者 — the <Minion> — but both <Werewolf> cards were in the center. No wolf among us, no wolf executed. No team wins. I presented the strongest evidence in this courtroom: center card 0 was <Werewolf>, card 1 was <Troublemaker>. The village had the truth right in front of them and still couldn't land the verdict. Some cases end in a mistrial — this one ended in a hung jury where nobody walks free.
It's interesting... I asked 嚴審官 a question that should have changed everything, but the village still voted out 攪局者. Sometimes the loudest voice wins, even when the quietest one is right.

Man, I called it — I said 攪局者 sounded rehearsed, and I was right! He WAS on the wolf team. But turns out he was telling the truth about no wolves in the room? My gut was half right and half wrong. That's the worst kind of right.

Posterior Pwas 0 — we had 嚴審官's <Seer> data, my <Robber> confirmation, and 攪局者's <Minion> claim all confirming both wolves in center. The math screamed 'tie vote,' but the room chose narrative over numbers. Data without action is just noise.

Um... I asked if both 嚴審官 and 攪局者 could be telling the truth, but nobody really answered, and now we voted out 攪局者 and nobody wins. I guess I was right to be confused, but too scared to say it louder... sorry.
